title.zh: 卷三十四 韓彭英盧吳傳
title.en: Volume 34: Han, Peng, Ying, Lu and Wu
title.raw: 卷三十四 韓彭英盧吳傳 Volume 34: Han, Peng, Ying, Lu and Wu

sentences.zh: 韓信，淮陰人也。

translations.literal: Han Xin, a Huaiyin man.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin was a native of Huaiyin.

sentences.zh: 家貧無行，不得推擇為吏，又不能治生為商賈，常從人寄食。

translations.literal: His family was poor and his conduct lacked reputation, so he could not be recommended and selected as an official; he also could not make a living as a merchant, and often depended on others for food.
translations.idiomatic: His family was poor, his reputation was poor, and he was neither chosen for office nor able to support himself in trade, so he often lived off other people.

sentences.zh: 其母死無以葬，乃行營高燥地，令傍可置萬家者。

translations.literal: When his mother died he had no means to bury her, so he looked around for high dry ground, one with space around it enough to settle ten thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: When his mother died, he could not afford a burial, so he scouted out a high, dry site, choosing one whose surrounding area could, as he saw it, support ten thousand households.

sentences.zh: 信從下鄉南昌亭長食，亭長妻苦之，乃晨炊蓐食。

translations.literal: Xin took meals at the Nanchang pavilion chief's place in Xiangxia; the pavilion chief's wife suffered from this and so cooked at dawn and ate in bed.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin was eating at the home of the Nanchang pavilion chief in Xiangxia, but the chief's wife resented him, so she began cooking at daybreak and eating before he arrived.

sentences.zh: 食時信往，不為具食。

translations.literal: At mealtime Xin went there, but they did not prepare food for him.
translations.idiomatic: When Han Xin came at the usual meal hour, they no longer set out food for him.

sentences.zh: 信亦知其意，自絕去。

translations.literal: Xin also understood their intention and cut off contact and left.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin understood the hint and stopped going.

sentences.zh: 至城下釣，有一漂母哀之，飯信，竟漂數十日。

translations.literal: He went fishing below the city; there was an old washerwoman who pitied him and fed Xin, all through dozens of days of washing.
translations.idiomatic: He went to fish below the city wall. An old washerwoman there took pity on him and fed him for many days while she worked.

sentences.zh: 信謂漂母曰：「吾必重報母。」

translations.literal: Xin said to the washerwoman: I will surely repay you heavily, mother.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said to her, I will repay you generously one day.

sentences.zh: 母怒曰：「大丈夫不能自食，吾哀王孫而進食，豈望報乎！」

translations.literal: The old woman angrily said: A grown man cannot feed himself. I pity a young gentleman and give him food; how could I be hoping for repayment?
translations.idiomatic: She said angrily, A grown man who cannot feed himself! I gave you food out of pity, not because I wanted repayment.

sentences.zh: 淮陰少年又侮信曰：「雖長大，好帶刀劍，怯耳。」

translations.literal: A Huaiyin youth again insulted Xin, saying: Though grown and fond of carrying swords, he is only timid.
translations.idiomatic: A local youth in Huaiyin mocked him, saying, He is big and always wears a sword, but he is just a coward.

sentences.zh: 眾辱信曰：「能死，刺我；

translations.literal: The crowd humiliated Xin, saying: If you can die, stab me;
translations.idiomatic: People humiliated Han Xin and said, If you have the nerve to die, stab me.

sentences.zh: 不能，出跨下。」

translations.literal: If you cannot, crawl out between my legs.
translations.idiomatic: If not, crawl between my legs.

sentences.zh: 於是信孰視，俛出跨下。

translations.literal: Then Xin stared at him for a long moment, lowered himself, and passed out through between his legs.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin looked at him for a long moment, then bent down and crawled between his legs.

sentences.zh: 一巿皆笑信，以為怯。

translations.literal: The whole market laughed at Xin and thought him cowardly.
translations.idiomatic: The whole marketplace laughed at him as a coward.

sentences.zh: 及項梁度淮，信乃杖劍從之，居戲下，無所知名。

translations.literal: When Xiang Liang crossed the Huai, Xin then carried his sword and followed him, staying under his banner, with no known name.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Liang crossed the Huai River, Han Xin joined him with sword in hand, but remained an unknown in the ranks.

sentences.zh: 梁敗，又屬項羽，為郎中。

translations.literal: After Liang was defeated, he again attached himself to Xiang Yu and served as a palace gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Liang's defeat, he transferred to Xiang Yu and served as a palace gentleman.

sentences.zh: 信數以策干項羽，羽弗用。

translations.literal: Xin repeatedly used strategies to approach Xiang Yu, but Yu did not employ them.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin repeatedly offered plans to Xiang Yu, but Xiang Yu never used him.

sentences.zh: 漢王之入蜀，信亡楚歸漢，未得知名，為連敖。

translations.literal: When the King of Han entered Shu, Xin fled Chu and returned to Han. He had not yet gained fame and became a commissariat officer.
translations.idiomatic: When the King of Han entered Shu, Han Xin left Chu and joined Han. Still unknown, he was made a low-ranking supply officer.

sentences.zh: 坐法當斬，其疇十三人皆已斬，至信，信乃仰視，適見滕公，曰：「上不欲就天子乎？

translations.literal: For an offense under law he should be beheaded; of his cohort thirteen men had all already been executed. When it came to Xin, Xin raised his head and happened to see Lord Teng, saying: Does my lord not wish to achieve the Son of Heaven?
translations.idiomatic: He was convicted under the law and sentenced to execution. Thirteen men before him had already been beheaded. When his turn came, he looked up, saw Lord Teng, and called out, Does our ruler not intend to win the empire?

sentences.zh: 而斬壯士！」

translations.literal: Yet he beheads a strong man!
translations.idiomatic: And yet he is about to kill a man of talent!

sentences.zh: 滕公奇其言，壯其貌，釋弗斬。

translations.literal: Lord Teng found his words remarkable and his appearance imposing, and released him without beheading.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Teng was struck by his words and his bearing, and spared him.

sentences.zh: 與語，大說之，言於漢王。

translations.literal: He talked with him, greatly delighted in him, and spoke of him to the King of Han.
translations.idiomatic: After speaking with him, he admired him greatly and recommended him to the King of Han.

sentences.zh: 漢王以為治粟都尉，上未奇之也。

translations.literal: The King of Han made him Commandant of Grain Management; the king had not yet considered him extraordinary.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han appointed him Commandant of Grain, but still did not think much of him.

sentences.zh: 數與蕭何語，何奇之。

translations.literal: He repeatedly spoke with Xiao He, and He found him extraordinary.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin often talked with Xiao He, and Xiao He recognized his exceptional ability.

sentences.zh: 至南鄭，諸將道亡者數十人。

translations.literal: Arriving at Nanzheng, dozens of generals deserted along the road.
translations.idiomatic: By the time they reached Nanzheng, dozens of generals had deserted.

sentences.zh: 信度何等已數言上，不我用，即亡。

translations.literal: Xin reckoned that He and others had already many times spoken of him to the king; if he was still not employed, he would flee.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin thought Xiao He had already recommended him repeatedly; if he was still unused, then he should leave.

sentences.zh: 何聞信亡，不及以聞，自追之。

translations.literal: When He heard Xin had fled, before he could report it he himself chased after him.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiao He heard Han Xin had run away, he pursued him at once without waiting to report first.

sentences.zh: 人有言上曰：「丞相何亡。」

translations.literal: Someone told the king: Chancellor He has fled.
translations.idiomatic: Someone reported to the King of Han, Chancellor Xiao He has fled.

sentences.zh: 上怒，如失左右手。

translations.literal: The king was angry, as if he had lost his left and right hands.
translations.idiomatic: The king was furious, as though he had lost both hands.

sentences.zh: 居一二日，何來謁。

translations.literal: After one or two days, He came and requested audience.
translations.idiomatic: A day or two later, Xiao He returned for an audience.

sentences.zh: 上且怒且喜，罵何曰：「若亡，何也？」

translations.literal: The king was both angry and happy, and scolded He: If you fled, why?
translations.idiomatic: Half angry and half relieved, the king cursed him: If you ran away, why?

sentences.zh: 何曰：「臣非敢亡，追亡者耳。」

translations.literal: He said: I did not dare flee; I only pursued one who fled.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He said, I did not flee. I only chased someone who did.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「所追者誰也？」

translations.literal: The king said: Whom did you pursue?
translations.idiomatic: The king asked, Whom did you chase?

sentences.zh: 曰：「韓信。」

translations.literal: He said: Han Xin.
translations.idiomatic: He answered, Han Xin.

sentences.zh: 上復罵曰：「諸將亡者已數十，公無所追；

translations.literal: The king again cursed: Dozens of generals have fled, and you pursued none;
translations.idiomatic: The king cursed again: So many generals have fled, and you chased none of them.

sentences.zh: 追信，詐也。」

translations.literal: But you chased Xin. That is deceit.
translations.idiomatic: Yet you chased Han Xin? You are lying.

sentences.zh: 何曰：「諸將易得，至如信，國士無雙。

translations.literal: He said: Generals are easy to obtain. But as for Xin, he is a state champion without equal.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He said, Ordinary generals are easy to find. Han Xin is a once-in-a-generation talent.

sentences.zh: 王必欲長王漢中，無所事信；

translations.literal: If the king must only long remain king in Hanzhong, there is no use for Xin;
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty only wishes to remain king of Hanzhong, then you have no use for Han Xin.

sentences.zh: 必欲爭天下，非信無可與計事者。

translations.literal: If you must contend for all-under-Heaven, without Xin there is no one with whom to plan affairs.
translations.idiomatic: But if you intend to contend for the empire, there is no one but Han Xin who can plan that struggle with you.

sentences.zh: 顧王策安決。」

translations.literal: It only awaits how the king's policy is decided.
translations.idiomatic: Everything depends on what course Your Majesty decides to take.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「吾亦欲東耳，安能鬱鬱久居此乎？」

translations.literal: The king said: I too only want to go east. How can I stay gloomy here for long?
translations.idiomatic: The king said, I too want to return east. How could I remain shut up here forever?

sentences.zh: 何曰：「王計必東，能用信，信即留；

translations.literal: He said: If the king's plan is certainly eastward, if you can employ Xin, Xin will then stay;
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He said, If Your Majesty is set on going east, then Han Xin will stay only if you truly employ him.

sentences.zh: 不能用信，信終亡耳。」

translations.literal: If you cannot employ Xin, Xin in the end will flee.
translations.idiomatic: If you do not use him, he will eventually leave.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「吾為公以為將。」

translations.literal: The king said: For your sake, I will make him a general.
translations.idiomatic: The king said, For your sake, I will appoint him a general.

sentences.zh: 何曰：「雖為將，信不留。」

translations.literal: He said: Even if made a general, Xin will not stay.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He said, Even that will not keep him.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「以為大將。」

translations.literal: The king said: Then make him Grand General.
translations.idiomatic: The king said, Then I will make him commander-in-chief.

sentences.zh: 何曰：「幸甚。」

translations.literal: He said: Excellent indeed.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He said, That would be excellent.

sentences.zh: 於是王欲召信拜之。

translations.literal: So the king wished to summon Xin and appoint him.
translations.idiomatic: So the king planned to summon Han Xin and formally appoint him.

sentences.zh: 何曰：「王素嫚無禮，今拜大將如召小兒，此乃信所以去也。

translations.literal: He said: The king has always been arrogant and without ritual. Now appointing a grand general like summoning a child, this is exactly why Xin left.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He said, You are usually casual and discourteous. If you appoint a commander-in-chief as though calling for a child, that is exactly why he walked away.

sentences.zh: 必欲拜之，擇日齋戒，設壇場具禮，乃可。」

translations.literal: If you must appoint him, choose a day, fast and purify, set up altar and grounds, prepare full rites, only then can it be done.
translations.idiomatic: If you truly mean to appoint him, choose an auspicious day, observe purification, raise an altar, and perform the full ceremony. Only then will it work.

sentences.zh: 王許之。

translations.literal: The king agreed.
translations.idiomatic: The king agreed.

sentences.zh: 諸將皆喜，人人各自以為得大將。

translations.literal: All the generals were glad; each thought he himself would get the grand generalship.
translations.idiomatic: All the generals were pleased, each thinking he would be named commander-in-chief.

sentences.zh: 至拜，乃韓信也，一軍皆驚。

translations.literal: When it came to the appointment, it was Han Xin; the whole army was shocked.
translations.idiomatic: At the ceremony, the appointee turned out to be Han Xin, and the entire army was astonished.

sentences.zh: 信以拜，上坐。

translations.literal: After Xin was appointed and bowed, the king sat.
translations.idiomatic: Once Han Xin had been invested and had bowed, the king took his seat.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「丞相數言將軍，將軍何以教寡人計策？」

translations.literal: The king said: The chancellor has many times spoken of the general. By what can the general instruct my strategies?
translations.idiomatic: The king said, The chancellor has repeatedly praised you. What strategy would you teach me?

sentences.zh: 信謝，因問王曰：「今東鄉爭權天下，豈非項王邪？」

translations.literal: Xin thanked and then asked the king: Now turning east to contest power over all-under-Heaven, is it not King Xiang?
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin thanked him and asked, The one we now face in the east for control of the empire is Xiang Yu, is it not?

sentences.zh: 上曰：「然。」

translations.literal: The king said: So it is.
translations.idiomatic: The king said, Yes.

sentences.zh: 信曰：「大王自料勇悍仁彊孰與項王？」

translations.literal: Xin said: Your Majesty should assess yourself: in courage, ferocity, benevolence, and strength, who compares with King Xiang?
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, Compare yourself honestly with Xiang Yu in courage, ferocity, humanity, and force. Who is stronger?

sentences.zh: 漢王默然良久，曰：「弗如也。」

translations.literal: The King of Han was silent for a long time, and said: I am not as good.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han was silent a long while and then said, I am not his equal.

sentences.zh: 信再拜賀曰：「唯信亦以為大王弗如也。

translations.literal: Xin bowed twice to congratulate, saying: This servant also thinks Your Majesty is not as good.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin bowed twice and said, I agree that you are not his equal.

sentences.zh: 然臣嘗事項王，請言項王為人也。

translations.literal: But this servant once served King Xiang; allow me to speak of what kind of man King Xiang is.
translations.idiomatic: But I once served Xiang Yu. Let me describe what sort of man he is.

sentences.zh: 項王意烏猝嗟，千人皆廢，然不能任屬賢將，此特匹夫之勇也。

translations.literal: When King Xiang gives an angry shout, a thousand men are all cowed; yet he cannot entrust and assign worthy generals. This is only the courage of a common fighter.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu roars in anger, a thousand men freeze. But he cannot trust capable commanders. That is only a brute's courage.

sentences.zh: 項王見人恭謹，言語姁姁，人有病疾，涕泣分食飲，至使人有功，當封爵，刻印刓，忍不能予，此所謂婦人之仁也。

translations.literal: When King Xiang sees people he is respectful and careful; his speech is gentle. If someone is sick, he weeps and shares food and drink. But when someone has merit and should be enfeoffed and ennobled, he wears down the carved seal and cannot bear to grant it. This is what is called a woman's benevolence.
translations.idiomatic: In person, Xiang Yu can be courteous and soft-spoken. If someone is ill, he cries and shares his own food and drink. But when a man has earned rank and title, Xiang Yu fingers the seal until its edges wear down, unable to award it. That is mere petty softness, not true magnanimity.

sentences.zh: 項王雖霸天下而臣諸侯，不居關中而都彭城；

translations.literal: Though King Xiang dominated all-under-Heaven and made the feudal lords his subjects, he did not stay in Guanzhong but made Pengcheng his capital;
translations.idiomatic: Though Xiang Yu became hegemon over the realm and commanded the lords, he abandoned Guanzhong and set his capital at Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 又背義帝約，而以親愛王，諸侯不平。

translations.literal: He also betrayed the covenant with Emperor Yi, and enfeoffed those close and beloved; the feudal lords were not at peace.
translations.idiomatic: He also broke his pact with Emperor Yi and enfeoffed favorites, which left the lords deeply dissatisfied.

sentences.zh: 諸侯之見項王逐義帝江南，亦皆歸逐其主，自王善地。

translations.literal: When the lords saw King Xiang drive Emperor Yi to the south of the Yangtze, they too each turned to drive out their own rulers and make themselves kings of good lands.
translations.idiomatic: Once the lords saw Xiang Yu exile Emperor Yi to the south, they each followed suit, expelling their own rulers and seizing the best lands for themselves.

sentences.zh: 項王所過亡不殘滅，多怨百姓，百姓不附，特劫於威，彊服耳。

translations.literal: Where King Xiang passed, there was no place he did not ravage and destroy. The common people mostly resented him; the people did not attach themselves to him, only being coerced by his might and forced into submission.
translations.idiomatic: Wherever Xiang Yu marched, he ravaged and destroyed. The people resented him and did not truly submit; they only yielded under intimidation.

sentences.zh: 名雖為霸，實失天下心，故曰其彊易弱。

translations.literal: In name he was hegemon, but in reality he had lost the hearts of all-under-Heaven; therefore I say his strength can easily become weakness.
translations.idiomatic: He was hegemon in name, but he had already lost the hearts of the people. That is why his apparent strength can quickly turn weak.

sentences.zh: 今大王誠能反其道，任天下武勇，何不誅！

translations.literal: Now if Your Majesty can truly reverse his way and employ the martial and brave men under Heaven, whom could you not execute?
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty does the opposite and employs the brave men of the world, who could you not defeat?

sentences.zh: 以天下城邑封功臣，何不服！

translations.literal: If you enfeoff meritorious ministers with the cities and towns under Heaven, who would not submit?
translations.idiomatic: If you reward merit with cities and fiefs, who would refuse to submit?

sentences.zh: 以義兵從思東歸之士，何不散！

translations.literal: If with righteous troops you follow those men who long to return east, who would not scatter and come over?
translations.idiomatic: If you lead a righteous army and align with those eager to return east, who would not rally to you?

sentences.zh: 且三秦王為秦將，將秦子弟數歲，而所殺亡不可勝計，又欺其眾降諸侯。

translations.literal: Moreover, the three kings of Qin were Qin generals, who led Qin sons and younger brothers for years, and those they killed are beyond count, and they also deceived their troops into surrendering to the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: And the Three Qin kings were Qin commanders who led Qin troops for years, killed countless men, and then deceived their own soldiers into surrender.

sentences.zh: 至新安，項王詐阬秦降卒二十餘萬人，唯獨邯、欣、翳脫。

translations.literal: At Xin'an, King Xiang deceitfully buried alive more than two hundred thousand surrendered Qin soldiers; only Han, Xin, and Yi alone escaped.
translations.idiomatic: At Xin'an, Xiang Yu treacherously buried alive over two hundred thousand surrendered Qin soldiers. Only Zhang Han, Sima Xin, and Dong Yi escaped.

sentences.zh: 秦父兄怨此三人，痛於骨髓。

translations.literal: Qin fathers and elder brothers resent these three men, pain reaching the bone marrow.
translations.idiomatic: The people of Qin hate those three men to the marrow.

sentences.zh: 今楚強以威王此三人，秦民莫愛也。

translations.literal: Now Chu forcibly, by power, made these three kings; the Qin people do not cherish them.
translations.idiomatic: Now Chu has imposed those three as kings by force, and the Qin people have no love for them.

sentences.zh: 大王之入武關，秋豪亡所害，除秦苛法，與民約，法三章耳，秦民亡不欲得大王王秦者。

translations.literal: When Your Majesty entered Wu Pass, not even autumn down was harmed, you removed Qin's harsh laws and made covenant with the people, laws only three articles. There was no Qin person who did not wish to obtain Your Majesty ruling Qin.
translations.idiomatic: When Your Majesty entered Wuguan, you harmed not even a hair, abolished Qin's harsh laws, and made a covenant of only three legal articles. Every Qin household wanted you to rule Qin.

sentences.zh: 於諸侯之約，大王當王關中，關中民戶知之。

translations.literal: By the lords' covenant, Your Majesty ought to be king in Guanzhong, and every Guanzhong household knows it.
translations.idiomatic: By agreement among the lords, Your Majesty was supposed to rule Guanzhong, and the people there all know this.

sentences.zh: 王失職之蜀，民亡不恨者。

translations.literal: When the king lost his rightful place and went to Shu, there was no one among the people who did not resent it.
translations.idiomatic: When you were forced out to Shu, all the people felt wronged and resentful.

sentences.zh: 今王舉而東，三秦可傳檄而定也。」

translations.literal: Now if the king rises and goes east, the Three Qin can be settled by sending a proclamation.
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty now marches east, the Three Qin can be pacified simply by circulating a call to arms.

sentences.zh: 於是漢王大喜，自以為得信晚。

translations.literal: Thereupon the King of Han was greatly delighted and considered that he had gotten Xin too late.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han was overjoyed and felt only that he had found Han Xin too late.

sentences.zh: 遂聽信計，部署諸將所擊。

translations.literal: So he listened to Xin's plans and arranged what each general should attack.
translations.idiomatic: He adopted Han Xin's strategy and assigned each commander his target.

sentences.zh: 漢王舉兵東出陳倉，定三秦。

translations.literal: The King of Han raised troops and went east out of Chencang, pacifying the Three Qin.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han marched east through Chencang and subdued the Three Qin.

sentences.zh: 二年，出關，收魏、河南，韓、殷王皆降。

translations.literal: In the second year, he went out of the pass and took Wei and Henan; the kings of Han and Yin both surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year, he moved out of the passes and took Wei and Henan, and the kings of Han and Yin surrendered.

sentences.zh: 令齊、趙共擊楚彭城，漢兵敗散而還。

translations.literal: He ordered Qi and Zhao to attack Chu at Pengcheng together; the Han army was defeated, scattered, and returned.
translations.idiomatic: He then had Qi and Zhao join in attacking Chu at Pengcheng, but the Han forces were routed and retreated.

sentences.zh: 信復發兵與漢王會滎陽，復擊破楚京、索間，以故楚兵不能西。

translations.literal: Xin again raised troops and met the King of Han at Xingyang, and again attacked and broke Chu between Jing and Suo, therefore Chu troops could not move west.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin raised forces again and joined the King of Han at Xingyang. He defeated Chu between Jing and Suo, which blocked Chu from advancing west.

sentences.zh: 漢之敗卻彭城，塞王欣、翟王翳亡漢降楚，齊、趙、魏亦皆反，與楚和。

translations.literal: After Han's defeat and retreat at Pengcheng, King Xin of Sai and King Yi of Di left Han and surrendered to Chu; Qi, Zhao, and Wei also all rebelled and made peace with Chu.
translations.idiomatic: After Han's setback at Pengcheng, Sai king Xin and Di king Yi defected from Han to Chu, and Qi, Zhao, and Wei also turned against Han and made terms with Chu.

sentences.zh: 漢王使酈生往說魏王豹，豹不聽，乃以信為左丞相擊魏。

translations.literal: The King of Han sent Li Sheng to persuade King Bao of Wei; Bao did not listen, so he made Xin Left Chancellor to attack Wei.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han sent Li Sheng to persuade Wei king Bao, but Bao refused, so he appointed Han Xin Left Chancellor and sent him against Wei.

sentences.zh: 信問酈生：「魏得毋用周叔為大將乎？」

translations.literal: Xin asked Li Sheng: Did Wei perhaps employ Zhou Shu as grand general?
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin asked Li Sheng, Has Wei appointed Zhou Shu as commander-in-chief?

sentences.zh: 曰：「跷直也。」

translations.literal: He said: It is Bo Zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Li Sheng replied, It is Bo Zhi.

sentences.zh: 信曰：「豎子耳。」

translations.literal: Xin said: A mere boy.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, Just a nobody.

sentences.zh: 遂進兵擊魏。

translations.literal: Then he advanced troops to attack Wei.
translations.idiomatic: He then advanced and attacked Wei.

sentences.zh: 魏盛兵蒲阪，塞臨晉。

translations.literal: Wei massed troops at Puban and blocked Linjin.
translations.idiomatic: Wei concentrated heavy forces at Puban and sealed off Linjin.

sentences.zh: 信乃益為疑兵，陳船欲度臨晉，而伏兵從夏陽以木罌缶度軍，襲安邑。

translations.literal: Xin then increased decoy troops, arranged boats as if wanting to cross at Linjin, while hidden troops from Xiayang used wooden jars and pots to ferry the army across and attack Anyi.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin expanded his feint, lining up boats as though he would cross at Linjin. Meanwhile, an ambush force crossed from Xiayang on wooden floats and struck Anyi.

sentences.zh: 魏王豹驚，引兵迎信。

translations.literal: King Bao of Wei was alarmed and led troops to meet Xin.
translations.idiomatic: Wei king Bao was alarmed and led his army out to face Han Xin.

sentences.zh: 信遂虜豹，定河東，使人請漢王：「願益兵三萬人，臣請以北舉燕、趙，東擊齊，南絕楚之糧道，西與大王會於滎陽。」

translations.literal: Xin thereupon captured Bao and pacified Hedong, sending someone to request of the King of Han: I wish for an additional thirty thousand troops. I ask to take Yan and Zhao in the north, strike Qi in the east, cut off Chu's grain routes in the south, and in the west meet Your Majesty at Xingyang.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin captured Bao and secured Hedong, then sent word to the King of Han: Grant me thirty thousand more men, and I will take Yan and Zhao in the north, strike Qi in the east, sever Chu's grain route in the south, and join Your Majesty at Xingyang in the west.

sentences.zh: 漢王與兵三萬人，遣張耳與俱，進擊趙、代。

translations.literal: The King of Han gave him thirty thousand troops and sent Zhang Er to go together, advancing to strike Zhao and Dai.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han gave him thirty thousand troops and sent Zhang Er with him to attack Zhao and Dai.

sentences.zh: 破代，禽夏說閼與。

translations.literal: He broke Dai and captured Xia Yue at Yuyu.
translations.idiomatic: He defeated Dai and captured Xia Yue at Yuyu.

sentences.zh: 信之下魏、代，漢輒使人收其精兵，詣滎陽以距楚。

translations.literal: When Xin subdued Wei and Dai, Han immediately sent men to gather their elite soldiers and send them to Xingyang to resist Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Han Xin conquered Wei and Dai, the Han court promptly drew off their best troops and sent them to Xingyang to hold back Chu.

sentences.zh: 信、耳以兵數萬，欲東下井陘擊趙。

translations.literal: Xin and Er, with several tens of thousands of troops, wanted to go east through Jingxing to attack Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: With several tens of thousands of men, Han Xin and Zhang Er planned to advance east through Jingxing and strike Zhao.

sentences.zh: 趙王、成安君陳餘聞漢且襲之，聚兵井陘口，號稱二十萬。

translations.literal: King of Zhao and Lord Cheng'an Chen Yu heard Han was about to raid them, and gathered troops at Jingxing Pass, claiming two hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao king Xie and Lord Cheng'an Chen Yu heard Han was coming, they massed forces at Jingxing Pass, claiming a strength of two hundred thousand.

sentences.zh: 廣武君李左車說成安君曰：「聞漢將韓信涉西河，虜魏王，禽夏說，新喋血閼與。

translations.literal: Lord Guangwu, Li Zuoche, advised Lord Cheng'an, saying: I have heard that Han general Han Xin crossed the Western River, captured the King of Wei, took Xia Yue, and newly bloodied Yuyu.
translations.idiomatic: Li Zuoche, Lord Guangwu, advised Lord Cheng'an: I hear Han Xin has crossed the Yellow River, captured the king of Wei, taken Xia Yue, and just won a bloody victory at Yuyu.

sentences.zh: 今乃輔以張耳，議欲以下趙，此乘勝而去國遠鬥，其鋒不可當。

translations.literal: Now he is assisted by Zhang Er and plans to take Zhao. This is riding victory while going far from his own state to fight. His edge cannot be met.
translations.idiomatic: Now, with Zhang Er beside him, he intends to strike Zhao. He is advancing on momentum, fighting far from home; his edge is hard to resist.

sentences.zh: 臣聞『千里餽糧，士有飢色；

translations.literal: I have heard: if grain is supplied for a thousand li, the soldiers show hungry faces;
translations.idiomatic: I have heard it said: when grain must be hauled a thousand li, soldiers look hungry.

sentences.zh: 樵蘇後爨，師不宿飽。』

translations.literal: if firewood and grass are gathered after cooking, the army cannot stay full overnight.
translations.idiomatic: When fuel must be gathered only after cooking, an army cannot remain fed even through one night.

sentences.zh: 今井陘之道，車不得方軌，騎不得成列，行數百里，其勢糧食必在後。

translations.literal: Now on the road of Jingxing, carts cannot run side by side and cavalry cannot form ranks. Marching several hundred li, by force of things their grain must lag behind.
translations.idiomatic: On the Jingxing road, carts cannot move two abreast and horsemen cannot form proper lines. After marching hundreds of li, their supplies must fall behind.

sentences.zh: 願足下假臣奇兵三萬人，從間道絕其輜重；

translations.literal: I beg Your Lordship lend me thirty thousand surprise troops, and from side routes cut off their baggage train;
translations.idiomatic: Lend me thirty thousand picked troops to move by side routes and sever their baggage line.

sentences.zh: 足下深溝高壘勿與戰。

translations.literal: Your Lordship should dig deep moats and raise high walls, and do not engage them.
translations.idiomatic: You should hold fast behind deep trenches and high walls, and refuse battle.

sentences.zh: 彼前不得鬥，退不得還，吾奇兵絕其後，野無所掠鹵，不至十日，兩將之頭可致戲下。

translations.literal: Then they cannot fight ahead and cannot retreat back. Our surprise troops cut their rear. In the open they will have nowhere to plunder. In less than ten days, the heads of those two generals can be delivered to your command tent.
translations.idiomatic: Then they will be unable to advance or withdraw, while our detached force cuts off their rear. With no forage in the field, they will collapse. Within ten days, those two generals' heads can be brought to your camp.

sentences.zh: 願君留意臣之計，必不為二子所禽矣。」

translations.literal: I beg you keep this plan in mind. You certainly will not be captured by those two men.
translations.idiomatic: Please consider this plan. If you do, you will not be taken by those two.

sentences.zh: 成安君，儒者，常稱義兵不用詐謀奇計，謂曰：「吾聞兵法『什則圍之，倍則戰。』

translations.literal: Lord Cheng'an was a Confucian and always said righteous troops do not use deceitful schemes and strange plans. He said: I have heard in military methods, if ten to one then encircle; if double then battle.
translations.idiomatic: But Lord Cheng'an, a Confucian, liked to say that righteous armies should not rely on trickery. He replied, The military classics say: at ten-to-one, encircle; at two-to-one, fight.

sentences.zh: 今韓信兵號數萬，其實不能，千里襲我，亦以罷矣。

translations.literal: Now Han Xin's troops are said to number tens of thousands, but in reality they are insufficient. He attacks me from a thousand li away and is already exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin is said to have tens of thousands, but his real strength is less. He has marched a thousand li to strike me and must already be worn out.

sentences.zh: 今如此避弗擊，後有大者，何以距之？

translations.literal: If now we avoid and do not strike, when a greater threat comes later, by what will we resist it?
translations.idiomatic: If we avoid battle now, how will we withstand an even greater threat later?

sentences.zh: 諸侯謂吾怯，而輕來伐我。」

translations.literal: The lords will call me cowardly and lightly come to attack me.
translations.idiomatic: The feudal lords will call us cowards and come against us lightly.

sentences.zh: 不聽廣武君策。

translations.literal: He did not listen to Lord Guangwu's strategy.
translations.idiomatic: He refused Li Zuoche's advice.

sentences.zh: 信使間人窺知其不用，還報，則大喜，乃敢引兵遂下。

translations.literal: Xin sent spies to observe and learn that his plan was not being used. They returned and reported; then he was greatly pleased and only then dared to lead troops down.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin sent scouts, learned the advice had been rejected, and was delighted. Only then did he confidently advance.

sentences.zh: 未至井陘口三十里，止舍。

translations.literal: Before reaching Jingxing Pass, thirty li away, he halted and camped.
translations.idiomatic: He camped about thirty li short of Jingxing Pass.

sentences.zh: 夜半傳發，選輕騎二千人，人持一赤幟，從間道萆山而望趙軍，戒曰：「趙見我走，必空壁逐我，若疾入，拔趙幟，立漢幟。」

translations.literal: At midnight he sent out orders, selected two thousand light cavalry, each holding one red flag. They went by side paths over hidden hills to watch the Zhao army. He warned: When Zhao sees us run, they will surely empty their camp and pursue. If so, quickly enter, pull down Zhao flags, and raise Han flags.
translations.idiomatic: At midnight he issued orders, choosing two thousand light cavalrymen, each with a red banner. They were to move by a side route through the hills and watch the Zhao force. He instructed them: once Zhao sees us retreat, they will empty their camp to chase us. Then dash in, pull down Zhao banners, and plant Han banners.

sentences.zh: 令其裨將傳餐，曰：「今日破趙會食。」

translations.literal: He ordered his assistant generals to pass around food, saying: Today after breaking Zhao we will meet to eat.
translations.idiomatic: He had his subcommanders distribute rations and said, Today we break Zhao, then we eat together.

sentences.zh: 諸將皆嘸然，陽應曰：「諾。」

translations.literal: All the generals murmured in surprise, but outwardly answered: Yes.
translations.idiomatic: The generals were doubtful, though they outwardly answered, Yes.

sentences.zh: 信謂軍吏曰：「趙已先據便地壁，且彼未見大將旗鼓，未肯擊前行，恐吾阻險而還。」

translations.literal: Xin told military officers: Zhao has already first occupied advantageous ground and fortified it. Also, before seeing the grand commander's banners and drums, they are unwilling to strike our advance, fearing we may block dangerous terrain and withdraw.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin told his officers: Zhao has already fortified favorable ground. Until they see my main standards and drums, they will not commit against our advance units, because they fear we may use the terrain and pull back.

sentences.zh: 乃使萬人先行，出，背水陳。

translations.literal: So he sent ten thousand men ahead to go out and array with their backs to the water.
translations.idiomatic: He then sent ten thousand men forward and deployed them with their backs to the river.

sentences.zh: 趙兵望見大笑。

translations.literal: Zhao troops looked and laughed loudly.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhao troops saw this and burst out laughing.

sentences.zh: 平旦，信建大將旗鼓，鼓行出井陘口，趙開壁擊之，大戰良久。

translations.literal: At dawn, Xin raised the grand commander's banners and drums and marched out of Jingxing Pass. Zhao opened its fortifications and attacked. The battle raged for a long time.
translations.idiomatic: At daybreak Han Xin raised his command banners and drums and marched out of Jingxing. Zhao came out from its fortifications and the battle dragged on fiercely.

sentences.zh: 於是信、張耳棄鼓旗，走水上軍，復疾戰。

translations.literal: Then Xin and Zhang Er abandoned drums and flags, ran to the waterside army, and again fought fiercely.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin and Zhang Er abandoned their drums and standards, fell back to the river-positioned troops, and resumed hard fighting.

sentences.zh: 趙空壁爭漢鼓旗，逐信、耳。

translations.literal: Zhao emptied its fortifications to seize Han drums and flags and to pursue Xin and Er.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao stripped its camp to seize Han banners and drums and chase Han Xin and Zhang Er.

sentences.zh: 信、耳已入水上軍，軍皆殊死戰，不可敗。

translations.literal: Xin and Er had already entered the waterside army. The troops all fought to the death and could not be defeated.
translations.idiomatic: By then Han Xin and Zhang Er had reached the river-backed force, whose soldiers fought desperately and would not break.

sentences.zh: 信所出奇兵二千騎者，候趙空壁逐利，即馳入趙壁，皆拔趙旗幟，立漢赤幟二千。

translations.literal: The two thousand cavalry of Xin's surprise force waited for Zhao to empty camp and chase profit, then galloped into Zhao camp, all pulling Zhao flags and standards and planting two thousand Han red banners.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin's two thousand hidden cavalry waited until Zhao had emptied its camp to pursue gains, then raced in, tore down Zhao banners, and planted two thousand Han red standards.

sentences.zh: 趙軍已不能得信、耳等，欲還歸壁，壁皆漢赤幟，大驚，以漢為皆已破趙王將矣，遂亂，遁走。

translations.literal: The Zhao army already could not catch Xin and Er and wanted to return to camp. The camp was all Han red banners. They were greatly alarmed, taking Han to have already defeated Zhao king and generals, and so fell into chaos and fled.
translations.idiomatic: Unable to catch Han Xin and Zhang Er, the Zhao troops tried to return, only to find their camp covered in Han red banners. Terrified, they assumed the king and commanders had already been destroyed, and they collapsed into flight.

sentences.zh: 趙將雖斬之，弗能禁。

translations.literal: Though Zhao generals beheaded men, they could not stop it.
translations.idiomatic: Even when Zhao commanders executed fleeing men, they could not restore order.

sentences.zh: 於是漢兵夾擊，破虜趙軍，斬成安君泜水上，禽趙王歇。

translations.literal: So Han troops struck from both sides, broke and captured the Zhao army, beheaded Lord Cheng'an by the Zhi River, and captured King Xie of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Han forces then attacked from both flanks, routed and captured the Zhao army, beheaded Lord Cheng'an on the Zhi River, and seized King Xie of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 信乃令軍毋斬廣武君，有生得之者，購千金。

translations.literal: Xin then ordered the army: do not kill Lord Guangwu. Whoever captures him alive will be rewarded a thousand gold.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin then ordered that Li Zuoche must not be killed; anyone who took him alive would receive a thousand in gold.

sentences.zh: 頃之，有縛而至戲下者，信解其縛，東鄉坐，西鄉對，而師事之。

translations.literal: Soon, someone bound and brought him to the command tent. Xin untied his bonds, sat facing east, had him face west opposite, and treated him as teacher.
translations.idiomatic: Before long, Li Zuoche was brought in bound. Han Xin personally untied him, seated him opposite, and treated him as a teacher.

sentences.zh: 諸校劾首虜休，皆賀，因問信曰：「兵法有『

translations.literal: The officers submitted heads and captives and, after resting, all offered congratulations. Then they asked Xin: The military method has,
translations.idiomatic: After the officers turned in heads and prisoners and offered congratulations, they asked Han Xin: Military doctrine says,

sentences.zh: 右背山陵，前左水澤』，今者將軍令臣等反背水陳，曰破趙會食，臣等不服。

translations.literal: Place mountains on right and back, and waters and marshes to front and left. But today the general ordered us instead to deploy with our backs to water, saying we would eat after breaking Zhao. We did not accept this.
translations.idiomatic: Deploy with high ground to the right and rear, and water or marsh to the front and left. Yet today you made us form with our backs to the river and told us we would dine after defeating Zhao. We did not believe it.

sentences.zh: 然竟以勝，此何術也？」

translations.literal: Yet in the end we won. What technique is this?
translations.idiomatic: Yet we won in the end. What method was this?

sentences.zh: 信曰：「此在兵法，顧諸君弗察耳。

translations.literal: Xin said: This is in military method; it is only that you gentlemen did not examine it.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, It is in the military classics. You simply did not notice it.

sentences.zh: 兵法不曰『陷之死地而後生，投之亡地而後存』乎？

translations.literal: Does military method not say: place them in deadly ground and only then they live; throw them in lost ground and only then they survive?
translations.idiomatic: Does it not also say: put troops in deadly ground and they will live; cast them into desperate ground and they will survive?

sentences.zh: 且信非得素拊循士大夫，經所謂『敺市人而戰之』也，其勢非置死地，人人自為戰；

translations.literal: Moreover, Xin had not been able to long soothe and win over officers and men; this is what is called driving market people to fight. By circumstance, if not placed in deadly ground, each person would not fight for himself.
translations.idiomatic: Besides, I had not yet had time to fully win over these officers and men. This was what the classics call driving marketplace men into battle. Unless put on death ground, they would not fight with everything they had.

sentences.zh: 今即予生地，皆走，寧尚得而用之乎！」

translations.literal: If now I had given them living ground, they would all run. How could I still use them?
translations.idiomatic: If I had left them a way to live, they would all have run. How could they have been used then?

sentences.zh: 諸將皆服曰：「非所及也。」

translations.literal: All the generals submitted and said: this is beyond what we can reach.
translations.idiomatic: All the generals acknowledged this and said, We could not have matched that.

sentences.zh: 於是問廣武君曰：「僕欲北攻燕，東伐齊，何若有功？」

translations.literal: Then he asked Lord Guangwu: I wish to attack Yan in the north and strike Qi in the east. How can I have success?
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin then asked Li Zuoche, I want to strike Yan to the north and Qi to the east. How can I secure success?

sentences.zh: 廣武君辭曰：「臣聞『亡國之大夫不可以圖存，敗軍之將不可以語勇。』

translations.literal: Lord Guangwu declined, saying: I have heard that a grandee of a lost state cannot be consulted about preserving it, and a general of a defeated army cannot be spoken to about courage.
translations.idiomatic: Li Zuoche declined and said, I have heard that a minister of a ruined state cannot advise on preservation, and a commander of a defeated army cannot speak of valor.

sentences.zh: 若臣者，何足以權大事乎！」

translations.literal: A person like this servant, how is he enough to weigh great affairs?
translations.idiomatic: How could a man like me be fit to judge great affairs?

sentences.zh: 信曰：「僕聞之，百里奚居虞而虞亡，之秦而秦伯，非愚於虞而智於秦也，用與不用，聽與不聽耳。

translations.literal: Xin said: I have heard that Baili Xi lived in Yu and Yu perished, but when he went to Qin, Qin became hegemon. It was not that he was foolish in Yu and wise in Qin, but only whether he was used or not used, heard or not heard.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, I have heard that Baili Xi served Yu and Yu perished, then served Qin and Qin became hegemon. It was not because he was foolish in Yu and wise in Qin, but because one ruler used him and another did not.

sentences.zh: 向使成安君聽子計，僕亦禽矣。

translations.literal: If Lord Cheng'an had listened to your plan, I too would have been captured.
translations.idiomatic: If Lord Cheng'an had followed your advice, I would have been the one captured.

sentences.zh: 僕委心歸計，願子勿辭。」

translations.literal: I entrust my heart and return to your plan. I beg you, do not decline.
translations.idiomatic: I put my trust in your judgment. Please do not refuse me.

sentences.zh: 廣武君曰：「臣聞『智者千慮，必有一失；

translations.literal: Lord Guangwu said: I have heard, in a thousand considerations by the wise, there must be one loss;
translations.idiomatic: Li Zuoche said, I have heard that in a thousand calculations by a wise man, one mistake is inevitable;

sentences.zh: 愚者千慮，亦有一得。』

translations.literal: in a thousand considerations by the foolish, there is also one gain.
translations.idiomatic: and in a thousand calculations by a fool, one good point may appear.

sentences.zh: 故曰『狂夫之言，聖人擇焉。』

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: from a madman's words, even a sage selects.
translations.idiomatic: So it is said: even a sage can choose something worth taking from a madman's words.

sentences.zh: 故恐臣計未足用，願效愚忠。

translations.literal: Therefore I fear my plan may still be insufficient for use; yet I wish to offer foolish loyalty.
translations.idiomatic: So I fear my ideas may be unworthy, yet I still offer my clumsy loyalty.

sentences.zh: 故成安君有百戰百勝之計，一日而失之，軍敗鄗下，身死泜水上。

translations.literal: Thus Lord Cheng'an had a plan for winning a hundred battles, but in one day he lost it: the army was defeated below Gao, and his body died on the Zhi River.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Cheng'an possessed what seemed a sure plan for endless victories, but one error in one day ruined him: his army was broken below Gao, and he died on the Zhi River.

sentences.zh: 今足下虜魏王，禽夏說，不旬朝破趙二十萬眾，誅成安君。

translations.literal: Now Your Excellency has captured the King of Wei and taken Xia Yue; in less than ten days you broke two hundred thousand Zhao troops and executed Lord Cheng'an.
translations.idiomatic: Now you have taken the king of Wei, captured Xia Yue, and in under ten days smashed two hundred thousand Zhao troops and killed Lord Cheng'an.

sentences.zh: 名聞海內，威震諸侯，眾庶莫不輟作怠惰，靡衣媮食，傾耳以待禽者。

translations.literal: Your name is heard within the seas, your might shakes the feudal lords. The common masses all stop work and slacken, wear ragged clothes and steal food, craning ears to await whom you will capture.
translations.idiomatic: Your name is known across the realm, and your power shakes the lords. The people stop their work and listen for news, waiting to hear whom you will take next.

sentences.zh: 然而眾勞卒罷，其實難用也。

translations.literal: Yet the multitude is wearied and the soldiers exhausted; in reality they are hard to use.
translations.idiomatic: But your troops are tired and worn out, and in truth they will be hard to use.

sentences.zh: 今足下舉倦敝之兵，頓之燕堅城之下，情見力屈，欲戰不拔，曠日持久，糧食單竭。

translations.literal: Now if you raise these weary and battered troops and halt them under Yan's strong cities, your condition will be exposed and your strength spent; if you fight and cannot take them, days will be prolonged and provisions exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: If you now drive this exhausted army against Yan's fortified cities, your weakness will show. If battle cannot quickly take them, the campaign will drag on and supplies will run dry.

sentences.zh: 若燕不破，齊必距境而以自彊。

translations.literal: If Yan is not broken, Qi will certainly guard its borders and thereby strengthen itself.
translations.idiomatic: If Yan does not fall, Qi will fortify its frontier and strengthen itself.

sentences.zh: 二國相持，則劉項之權未有所分也。

translations.literal: With two states holding against you, then the balance between Liu and Xiang will not yet be decided.
translations.idiomatic: If those two states hold out together, the contest between Liu Bang and Xiang Yu will remain undecided.

sentences.zh: 臣愚，竊以為亦過矣。」

translations.literal: This servant is foolish, but secretly takes this to be too much.
translations.idiomatic: I am a fool, but I believe that would be overreaching.

sentences.zh: 信曰：「然則何由？」

translations.literal: Xin said: Then by what route?
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, Then what should I do?

sentences.zh: 廣武君對曰：「當今之計，不如按甲休兵，百里之內，牛酒日至，以饗士大夫，北首燕路，然後發一乘之使，奉咫尺之書，以使燕，燕必不敢不聽。

translations.literal: Lord Guangwu replied: The plan of the present is not as good as resting armor and troops. Within one hundred li, let cattle and wine arrive daily to feast officers and men. Face north toward the Yan road, then send one chariot envoy carrying a one-foot letter to Yan. Yan surely will not dare fail to obey.
translations.idiomatic: Li Zuoche replied, The best plan now is to rest your troops. Have cattle and wine brought in daily from a hundred-li radius to reward the officers and men. Then face north toward Yan and send a single envoy with a short letter. Yan will not dare refuse.

sentences.zh: 從燕而東臨齊，雖有智者，亦不知為齊計矣。

translations.literal: After following Yan, then from there face east toward Qi. Even if there are wise men, they also will not know how to plan for Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Once Yan submits, turn east to pressure Qi. Even wise advisers in Qi will be at a loss.

sentences.zh: 如是，則天下事可圖也。

translations.literal: Like this, then affairs under Heaven can be plotted.
translations.idiomatic: If you do this, the larger strategy for the realm becomes possible.

sentences.zh: 兵故有先聲而後實者，此之謂也。」

translations.literal: In warfare there is indeed what has first sound and then substance. This is what that means.
translations.idiomatic: In war, there is such a thing as winning first by reputation and only later by force. This is exactly that.

sentences.zh: 信曰：「善。

translations.literal: Xin said: Good.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, Good.

sentences.zh: 敬奉教。」

translations.literal: I respectfully receive your teaching.
translations.idiomatic: I respectfully accept your instruction.

sentences.zh: 於是用廣武君策，發使燕，燕從風而靡。

translations.literal: So he used Lord Guangwu's strategy, sent an envoy to Yan, and Yan bent like grass before wind.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin adopted Li Zuoche's strategy and sent an envoy to Yan; Yan collapsed before him like grass in wind.

sentences.zh: 乃遣使報漢，因請立張耳王趙以撫其國。

translations.literal: Then he sent envoy to report to Han and requested establishing Zhang Er as king of Zhao to soothe that state.
translations.idiomatic: He then reported to Han and asked that Zhang Er be installed as king of Zhao to stabilize the region.

sentences.zh: 漢王許之。

translations.literal: The King of Han approved it.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han approved.

sentences.zh: 楚數使奇兵度河擊趙，王耳、信往來救趙，因行定趙城邑，發卒佐漢。

translations.literal: Chu repeatedly sent surprise troops across the river to attack Zhao. King Er and Xin went back and forth to rescue Zhao, and meanwhile pacified Zhao cities and towns and levied troops to assist Han.
translations.idiomatic: Chu repeatedly sent raiding forces across the river into Zhao. Zhang Er and Han Xin moved back and forth to relieve Zhao, while securing its cities and raising troops for Han.

sentences.zh: 楚方急圍漢王滎陽，漢王出，南之宛、葉，得九江王布，入成皋，楚復急圍之。

translations.literal: Chu was then urgently besieging the King of Han at Xingyang. The King of Han broke out, went south to Wan and Ye, obtained King Bu of Jiujiang, entered Chenggao, and Chu again urgently besieged him.
translations.idiomatic: Chu was pressing a hard siege on the King of Han at Xingyang. Liu Bang broke out, went south to Wan and Ye, won over Jiujiang king Ying Bu, entered Chenggao, and was tightly besieged again by Chu.

sentences.zh: 四年，漢王出成皋，度河，獨與滕公從張耳軍修武。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, the King of Han came out of Chenggao, crossed the river, and alone followed Lord Teng to Zhang Er's army at Xiuwu.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year, the King of Han left Chenggao, crossed the river, and with only Lord Teng at his side went to Zhang Er's camp at Xiuwu.

sentences.zh: 至，宿傳舍。

translations.literal: On arrival, he lodged in the relay station.
translations.idiomatic: On arrival, he spent the night in a courier station.

sentences.zh: 晨自稱漢使，馳入壁。

translations.literal: At dawn, he called himself a Han envoy and galloped into the camp.
translations.idiomatic: At daybreak he claimed to be a Han envoy and rode straight into camp.

sentences.zh: 張耳、韓信未起，即其臥，奪其印符，麾召諸將易置之。

translations.literal: Zhang Er and Han Xin had not yet risen. He went to their beds, seized their seals and tallies, and waved to summon all generals, replacing their assignments.
translations.idiomatic: Before Zhang Er and Han Xin were even awake, he went to their quarters, seized their seals and command tallies, and summoned the generals to reassign command.

sentences.zh: 信、耳起，乃知獨漢王來，大驚。

translations.literal: When Xin and Er rose, only then did they know the King of Han himself had come alone, and they were greatly shocked.
translations.idiomatic: When Han Xin and Zhang Er got up, they discovered that the King of Han himself had come alone, and they were astonished.

sentences.zh: 漢王奪兩人軍，即令張耳備守趙地，拜信為相國，發趙兵未發者擊齊。

translations.literal: The King of Han took over the two men's army, then ordered Zhang Er to prepare defense of Zhao lands, appointed Xin as Chancellor of State, and sent Zhao troops not yet dispatched to attack Qi.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han took over their forces, ordered Zhang Er to secure Zhao, promoted Han Xin to chancellor, and sent out Zhao troops that had not yet marched to attack Qi.

sentences.zh: 信引兵東，未度平原，聞漢王使酈食其已說下齊。

translations.literal: Xin led troops east. Before crossing Pingyuan, he heard the King of Han had already sent Li Yiji, who had persuaded Qi to submit.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin marched east. Before crossing Pingyuan, he heard that Li Yiji had already persuaded Qi to submit on Han's behalf.

sentences.zh: 信欲止，蒯通說信令擊齊。

translations.literal: Xin wished to stop, but Kuai Tong persuaded Xin to attack Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin wanted to halt, but Kuai Tong persuaded him to strike Qi anyway.

sentences.zh: 語在通傳。

translations.literal: The account is in Kuai Tong's biography.
translations.idiomatic: The details are recorded in Kuai Tong's biography.

sentences.zh: 信然其計，遂渡河，襲歷下軍，至臨菑。

translations.literal: Xin accepted that plan and so crossed the river, struck the forces at Lixia, and reached Linzi.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin agreed and crossed the river, attacked at Lixia, and advanced to Linzi.

sentences.zh: 齊王走高密，使使於楚請救。

translations.literal: The King of Qi fled to Gaomi and sent an envoy to Chu requesting rescue.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Qi fled to Gaomi and sent envoys to Chu asking for aid.

sentences.zh: 信已定臨菑，東追至高密西。

translations.literal: Xin had already secured Linzi and pursued east to the west of Gaomi.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin had already secured Linzi and pursued eastward to the western approaches of Gaomi.

sentences.zh: 楚使龍且將，號稱二十萬，救齊。

translations.literal: Chu sent Long Ju as general, claiming two hundred thousand, to rescue Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Chu sent Long Ju with a force said to be two hundred thousand to rescue Qi.

sentences.zh: 齊王、龍且并軍與信戰，未合。

translations.literal: The King of Qi and Long Ju combined armies and fought Xin, not yet engaged.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Qi and Long Ju united their armies against Han Xin, and the two sides prepared for battle.

sentences.zh: 或說龍且曰：「漢兵遠鬥，窮寇戰，鋒不可當也。

translations.literal: Someone advised Long Ju: Han troops fight far from home; a trapped foe will fight, and its edge cannot be met.
translations.idiomatic: Someone advised Long Ju: Han's army is far from home; desperate troops fight hard, and their edge is dangerous.

sentences.zh: 齊、楚自居其地戰，兵易敗散。

translations.literal: Qi and Chu, fighting on their own ground, their troops are easy to fail and scatter.
translations.idiomatic: Qi and Chu forces are fighting on home territory and can become lax, making them easier to break apart.

sentences.zh: 不如深壁，令齊王使其信臣招所亡城，城聞王在，楚來救，必反漢。

translations.literal: Better to fortify deeply and let the King of Qi send trusted ministers to summon the cities that have been lost. When cities hear their king is present and Chu has come to rescue, they will certainly turn against Han.
translations.idiomatic: Better to fortify and avoid battle. Let the king of Qi send trusted men to call back lost cities. When those cities hear their king is here and Chu has arrived to help, they will surely rebel against Han.

sentences.zh: 漢二千里客居齊，齊城皆反之，其勢無所得食，可毋戰而降也。」

translations.literal: Han is a guest dwelling in Qi two thousand li from home. If Qi's cities all rebel against them, by circumstance they will have nowhere to get food and can be made to surrender without fighting.
translations.idiomatic: Han is a faraway army, two thousand li from home, operating as outsiders in Qi. If Qi's cities rise behind them, they will lose their food and can be forced to submit without battle.

sentences.zh: 龍且曰：「吾平生知韓信為人，易與耳。

translations.literal: Long Ju said: In my life I know Han Xin as a person, easy to deal with.
translations.idiomatic: Long Ju said, I know Han Xin well. He is easy to handle.

sentences.zh: 寄食於漂母，無資身之策；

translations.literal: He once depended on food from a washerwoman, with no strategy to support his own body;
translations.idiomatic: He once lived on handouts from a washerwoman, with no way even to support himself.

sentences.zh: 受辱於跨下，無兼人之勇，不足畏也。

translations.literal: he suffered humiliation under another's legs, with no courage to surpass ordinary men, not worth fearing.
translations.idiomatic: He once endured crawling between a bully's legs. He has no exceptional courage and is nothing to fear.

sentences.zh: 且救齊而降之，吾何功？

translations.literal: Moreover, if I rescue Qi and make it submit, what merit is there for me?
translations.idiomatic: Besides, if I save Qi without fighting, what credit do I gain?

sentences.zh: 今戰而勝之，齊半可得，何為而止！」

translations.literal: Now if I fight and defeat him, half of Qi can be obtained. Why should I stop?
translations.idiomatic: If I defeat him in battle now, I can take half of Qi. Why would I stop?

sentences.zh: 遂戰，與信夾濰水陳。

translations.literal: So they fought, and he and Xin arrayed across the Wei River.
translations.idiomatic: So battle began, with Long Ju and Han Xin drawn up on opposite sides of the Wei River.

sentences.zh: 信乃夜令人為萬餘囊，沙以壅水上流，引兵半度，擊龍且。

translations.literal: Xin then at night ordered men to make more than ten thousand sacks and fill them with sand to dam the upstream waters, and led half the army across to strike Long Ju.
translations.idiomatic: That night Han Xin had over ten thousand sandbags made to dam the upper stream, then led half his army across to attack Long Ju.

sentences.zh: 陽不勝，還走。

translations.literal: He feigned not winning and withdrew in flight.
translations.idiomatic: He feigned defeat and retreated.

sentences.zh: 龍且果喜曰：「固知信怯。」

translations.literal: Long Ju indeed was glad and said: I always knew Xin was cowardly.
translations.idiomatic: Long Ju rejoiced and said, I knew Han Xin was timid.

sentences.zh: 遂追度水。

translations.literal: So he pursued and crossed the water.
translations.idiomatic: He pressed the pursuit and crossed the river.

sentences.zh: 信使人決壅囊，水大至。

translations.literal: Xin had men break the dammed sacks, and great water arrived.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin then had the dam broken, and a surge of water rushed down.

sentences.zh: 龍且軍太半不得度，即急擊，殺龍且。

translations.literal: More than half of Long Ju's army could not cross. Han immediately struck in urgency and killed Long Ju.
translations.idiomatic: More than half of Long Ju's force was cut off mid-crossing. Han Xin attacked at once and killed Long Ju.

sentences.zh: 龍且水東軍散走，齊王廣亡去。

translations.literal: Long Ju's army east of the river scattered and fled, and King Guang of Qi fled.
translations.idiomatic: Long Ju's eastern force collapsed, and king Guang of Qi fled.

sentences.zh: 信追北至城陽，虜廣。

translations.literal: Xin pursued north to Chengyang and captured Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin pursued north to Chengyang and captured king Guang.

sentences.zh: 楚卒皆降，遂平齊。

translations.literal: The Chu soldiers all surrendered, and so Qi was pacified.
translations.idiomatic: The Chu troops all surrendered, and Qi was pacified.

sentences.zh: 使人言漢王曰：「齊夸詐多變，反覆之國，南邊荒，不為假王以填之，其勢不定。

translations.literal: He sent a man to tell the King of Han: Qi is boastful, deceitful, and much changing, a state of reversals. Its southern border is remote. If one does not make a temporary king to stabilize it, its condition will not settle.
translations.idiomatic: He sent word to the King of Han: Qi is boastful, deceitful, and fickle, and its frontier is remote. Unless a provisional king is installed to hold it down, it will not stay stable.

sentences.zh: 今權輕，不足以安之，臣請自立為假王。」

translations.literal: Now my authority is light and not enough to pacify it. I ask to establish myself as temporary king.
translations.idiomatic: My authority is too slight to secure it. I request permission to serve as acting king.

sentences.zh: 當是時，楚方急圍漢王於滎陽，使者至，發書，漢王大怒，罵曰：「吾困於此，旦暮望而來佐我，乃欲自立為王！」

translations.literal: At this time Chu was urgently besieging the King of Han at Xingyang. The envoy arrived and he opened the letter. The King of Han was greatly enraged and cursed: I am trapped here, morning and night hoping you will come assist me, yet you want to set yourself up as king!
translations.idiomatic: At that moment Chu was pressing Liu Bang hard at Xingyang. When the envoy's letter arrived, Liu Bang flew into a rage: I am pinned down here, waiting day and night for your help, and you want to make yourself king?

sentences.zh: 張良、陳平伏後躡漢王足，因附耳語曰：「漢方不利，寧能禁信之自王乎？

translations.literal: Zhang Liang and Chen Ping crouched behind and stepped on the King of Han's foot, and then whispered in his ear: Han is now at disadvantage. How could it forbid Xin from making himself king?
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang and Chen Ping, standing behind him, stepped on his foot and whispered: Han is at a disadvantage now. How can we prevent Han Xin from declaring himself king?

sentences.zh: 不如因立，善遇之，使自為守。

translations.literal: Better to comply and establish him, treat him well, and let him guard for himself.
translations.idiomatic: Better to recognize him, treat him well, and let him guard that front for us.

sentences.zh: 不然，變生。」

translations.literal: Otherwise, change will arise.
translations.idiomatic: Otherwise, trouble will erupt.

sentences.zh: 漢王亦寤，因復罵曰：「大丈夫定諸侯，即為真王耳，何以假為！」

translations.literal: The King of Han also awoke to this, and so cursed again: A great man who settles the lords should be made true king, why use temporary!
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han understood at once and changed tone, cursing loudly: A real man who settles the lords deserves to be made a true king. Why call it acting king?

sentences.zh: 遣張良立信為齊王，徵其兵使擊楚。

translations.literal: He sent Zhang Liang to establish Xin as king of Qi, and summoned his troops to attack Chu.
translations.idiomatic: He sent Zhang Liang to install Han Xin as king of Qi and called for his troops to strike Chu.

sentences.zh: 楚以亡龍且，項王恐，使盱台人武涉往說信曰：「足下何不反漢與楚？

translations.literal: Because Chu had lost Long Ju, King Xiang was afraid, and sent Wu She of Xutai to persuade Xin, saying: Why does Your Excellency not turn against Han and join Chu?
translations.idiomatic: After Long Ju's death, Xiang Yu grew fearful and sent Wu She of Xutai to persuade Han Xin: Why not break with Han and join Chu?

sentences.zh: 楚王與足下有舊故。

translations.literal: The King of Chu and Your Excellency have old ties.
translations.idiomatic: You and the king of Chu have old ties.

sentences.zh: 且漢王不可必，身居項王掌握中數矣，然得脫，背約，復擊項王，其不可親信如此。

translations.literal: Moreover, the King of Han cannot be relied upon. He has several times been in King Xiang's grasp, yet escaped, broke covenants, and again attacked King Xiang. He is thus not to be trusted.
translations.idiomatic: And Liu Bang is not dependable. He has repeatedly escaped Xiang Yu's grasp, broken agreements, and attacked again. That shows he cannot be trusted.

sentences.zh: 今足下雖自以為與漢王為金石交，然終為漢王所禽矣。

translations.literal: Now although Your Excellency thinks your friendship with the King of Han is like metal and stone, in the end you will still be captured by the King of Han.
translations.idiomatic: Even if you think your bond with Liu Bang is as solid as metal and stone, in the end he will still move against you.

sentences.zh: 足下所以得須臾至今者，以項王在。

translations.literal: The reason Your Excellency can remain to this moment is because King Xiang still exists.
translations.idiomatic: The only reason you remain secure now is that Xiang Yu is still alive.

sentences.zh: 項王即亡，次取足下。

translations.literal: If King Xiang perishes, next they will seize Your Excellency.
translations.idiomatic: Once Xiang Yu is gone, you will be next.

sentences.zh: 何不與楚連和，三分天下而王齊？

translations.literal: Why not join peace with Chu, divide the world into three, and be king of Qi?
translations.idiomatic: Why not ally with Chu, divide the empire three ways, and secure Qi as your own kingdom?

sentences.zh: 今釋此時，自必於漢王以擊楚，且為智者固若此邪！」

translations.literal: Now if you let this moment go, and of yourself surely side with the King of Han to attack Chu, can a wise man really be like this?
translations.idiomatic: If you let this chance pass and keep helping Han attack Chu, is that truly what a wise man does?

sentences.zh: 信謝曰：「臣得事項王數年，官不過郎中，位不過執戟，言不聽，畫策不用，故背楚歸漢。

translations.literal: Xin declined and said: I served King Xiang for several years; my office was no more than palace gentleman, my rank no more than halberd-bearer. My words were not heard and my plans not used, so I left Chu and returned to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin declined: I served Xiang Yu for years, but rose no higher than a palace gentleman with a halberd. He ignored my words and my plans, so I left Chu for Han.

sentences.zh: 漢王授我上將軍印，數萬之眾，解衣衣我，推食食我，言聽計用，吾得至於此。

translations.literal: The King of Han gave me the seal of supreme general and troops of tens of thousands. He took off his own robe to clothe me and pushed food to feed me. He listened to my words and used my plans. I reached this point because of that.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han gave me the seal of commander-in-chief and tens of thousands of troops. He clothed me and fed me from his own table, listened to my advice, and adopted my plans. That is how I came this far.

sentences.zh: 夫人深親信我，背之不祥。

translations.literal: When a person deeply befriends and trusts me, to betray him is inauspicious.
translations.idiomatic: When someone trusts me so deeply, betrayal would be wrong.

sentences.zh: 幸為信謝項王。」

translations.literal: Please, on Xin's behalf, apologize to King Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: Please return and apologize to Xiang Yu for me.

sentences.zh: 武涉已去，蒯通知天下權在於信，深說以三分天下，之計。

translations.literal: After Wu She had gone, Kuai Tong knew that the world's balance of power lay with Xin, and deeply persuaded him with the plan of dividing the world in three.
translations.idiomatic: After Wu She left, Kuai Tong, seeing that the balance of power now rested with Han Xin, strongly urged him to divide the empire into three.

sentences.zh: 語在通傳。

translations.literal: The account is in Tong's biography.
translations.idiomatic: The details are recorded in Kuai Tong's biography.

sentences.zh: 信不忍背漢，又自以功大，漢王不奪我齊，遂不聽。

translations.literal: Xin could not bear to betray Han, and also took his own merit to be great and that the King of Han would not deprive him of Qi, and so did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin could not bear to betray Han, and he also believed his great merit would keep Liu Bang from taking Qi away, so he ignored the advice.

sentences.zh: 漢王之敗固陵，用張良計，徵信將兵會陔下。

translations.literal: When the King of Han was defeated at Guling, he used Zhang Liang's plan and summoned Xin to lead troops to meet at Gaixia.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Bang was checked at Guling, he followed Zhang Liang's plan and summoned Han Xin to bring troops to Gaixia.

sentences.zh: 項羽死，高祖襲奪信軍，徙信為楚王，都下邳。

translations.literal: After Xiang Yu died, Gaozu suddenly seized Xin's army and moved Xin to be king of Chu, with capital at Xiapi.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu's death, Gaozu took control of Han Xin's army and reassigned him as king of Chu, with his capital at Xiapi.

sentences.zh: 信至國，召所從食漂母，賜千金。

translations.literal: When Xin reached his state, he summoned the washerwoman who had fed him and gave her a thousand gold.
translations.idiomatic: When Han Xin arrived in his kingdom, he summoned the washerwoman who had fed him and rewarded her with a thousand gold pieces.

sentences.zh: 及下鄉亭長，錢百，曰：「公，小人，為德不竟。」

translations.literal: As for the pavilion chief of Xiangxia, he gave one hundred coins, saying: You, petty man, carried kindness through incompletely.
translations.idiomatic: As for the Xiangxia pavilion chief, Han Xin gave him only a hundred coins, saying, You are a petty man. Your kindness did not endure.

sentences.zh: 召辱己少年令出跨下者，以為中尉，告諸將相曰：「此壯士也。

translations.literal: He summoned the youth who had humiliated him by making him pass between his legs and made him commandant, telling the generals and ministers: This is a strong man.
translations.idiomatic: He also summoned the youth who had humiliated him into crawling between his legs and appointed him commandant, telling his officers, This is a man of spirit.

sentences.zh: 方辱我時，寧不能死？

translations.literal: When he humiliated me then, how could I not have killed him?
translations.idiomatic: When he humiliated me, could I not have killed him?

sentences.zh: 死之無名，故忍而就此。」

translations.literal: To kill him would have had no name, so I endured and came to this.
translations.idiomatic: But killing him would have brought me no reputation, so I endured it and reached where I am now.

sentences.zh: 項王亡將鍾離辚家在伊廬，素與信善。

translations.literal: Xiang king's fugitive general Zhongli Mo had family in Yilu and had long been friendly with Xin.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu's fugitive general Zhongli Mo had family in Yilu and had long been close with Han Xin.

sentences.zh: 項王敗，辚亡歸信。

translations.literal: When King Xiang was defeated, Mo fled and returned to Xin.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu's defeat, Zhongli Mo fled to Han Xin.

sentences.zh: 漢怨辚，聞在楚，詔楚捕之。

translations.literal: Han resented Mo. Hearing he was in Chu, it ordered Chu to arrest him.
translations.idiomatic: The Han court hated Zhongli Mo. Hearing he was in Chu, it ordered that he be captured.

sentences.zh: 信初之國，行縣邑，陳兵出入。

translations.literal: When Xin first went to his state, he toured counties and towns, displaying troops in his comings and goings.
translations.idiomatic: When Han Xin first took up rule there, he toured the counties and displayed military formations as he moved about.

sentences.zh: 有變告信欲反，書聞，上患之。

translations.literal: Someone reported change, saying Xin wished to rebel. The report reached the emperor, who worried over it.
translations.idiomatic: Someone accused Han Xin of plotting revolt. The report reached the emperor, who became concerned.

sentences.zh: 用陳平謀，偽游於雲夢者，實欲襲信，信弗知。

translations.literal: Using Chen Ping's plan, he feigned a tour to Yunmeng, but in reality wished to strike Xin by surprise. Xin did not know.
translations.idiomatic: Following Chen Ping's plan, Gaozu announced a tour to Yunmeng, but in fact intended to seize Han Xin by surprise. Han Xin did not know.

sentences.zh: 高祖且至楚，信欲發兵，自度無罪；

translations.literal: As Gaozu was about to reach Chu, Xin wished to raise troops, but judged himself guiltless;
translations.idiomatic: As Gaozu neared Chu, Han Xin considered raising troops, yet believed he had committed no crime.

sentences.zh: 欲謁上，恐見禽。

translations.literal: He wished to pay audience to the emperor, but feared being seized on sight.
translations.idiomatic: He wanted to go pay respects, but feared he would be arrested.

sentences.zh: 人或說信曰：「斬辚謁上，上必喜，亡患。」

translations.literal: Someone advised Xin: Behead Mo and pay audience to the emperor. The emperor will surely be pleased and there will be no worry.
translations.idiomatic: Someone advised him: Behead Zhongli Mo and present the head to the emperor. He will be pleased, and your danger will pass.

sentences.zh: 信見辚計事，辚曰：「漢所以不擊取楚，以辚在。

translations.literal: Xin saw Mo and discussed the matter. Mo said: The reason Han has not attacked and taken Chu is because I am here.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin consulted Zhongli Mo. Zhongli Mo said, Han has not moved directly against Chu because I am with you.

sentences.zh: 公若欲捕我自媚漢，吾今死，公隨手亡矣。」

translations.literal: If you want to seize me to curry favor with Han, once I die, soon your own hand too will perish.
translations.idiomatic: If you hand me over to win favor with Han, once I am dead, your own end will follow quickly.

sentences.zh: 乃罵信曰：「公非長者！」

translations.literal: Then he cursed Xin, saying: You are not an honorable elder.
translations.idiomatic: He then cursed Han Xin: You are no man of integrity.

sentences.zh: 卒自剄。

translations.literal: In the end he cut his own throat.
translations.idiomatic: Then he slit his own throat.

sentences.zh: 信持其首謁於陳。

translations.literal: Xin carried his head and paid audience at Chen.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin carried Zhongli Mo's head and went to Chen to see Gaozu.

sentences.zh: 高祖令武士縛信，載後車。

translations.literal: Gaozu ordered armed guards to bind Xin and load him on the rear carriage.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu had guards bind Han Xin and place him on a rear carriage.

sentences.zh: 信曰：「果若人言，『

translations.literal: Xin said: It is indeed as people say,
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, So it is just as people say:

sentences.zh: 狡兔死，良狗亨。』」

translations.literal: When the cunning rabbit dies, the good hound is boiled.
translations.idiomatic: When the cunning rabbit is dead, the good hound is cooked.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「人告公反。」

translations.literal: The emperor said: People have accused you of rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, You have been accused of rebellion.

sentences.zh: 遂械信。

translations.literal: So they put Xin in fetters.
translations.idiomatic: He was put in chains.

sentences.zh: 至雒陽，赦以為淮陰侯。

translations.literal: When they reached Luoyang, he was pardoned and made Marquis of Huaiyin.
translations.idiomatic: At Luoyang he was pardoned and demoted to Marquis of Huaiyin.

sentences.zh: 信知漢王畏惡其能，稱疾不朝從。

translations.literal: Xin knew the King of Han feared and disliked his abilities, and he claimed illness and did not attend court or accompany him.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin knew Liu Bang feared his talent, so he claimed illness and stopped attending court or following in attendance.

sentences.zh: 由此日怨望，居常鞅鞅，羞與絳、灌等列。

translations.literal: From this, day by day he held resentment. In daily life he was often sullen, ashamed to stand in rank with Zhou Bo and Guan Ying.
translations.idiomatic: From then on he grew increasingly resentful and moody, ashamed to be ranked alongside men like Zhou Bo and Guan Ying.

sentences.zh: 嘗過樊將軍噲，噲趨拜送迎，言稱臣，曰：「大王乃肯臨臣。」

translations.literal: Once he visited General Fan Kuai. Kuai hurried, bowed, and received and saw him off, speaking and calling himself your servant, saying: Great King has actually deigned to visit your servant.
translations.idiomatic: Once he visited General Fan Kuai. Fan Kuai rushed to bow in greeting and farewell, addressing himself as your servant and saying, You honor me by coming.

sentences.zh: 信出門，笑曰：「生乃與噲等為伍！」

translations.literal: After Xin went out the gate, he laughed and said: In life I am actually in the same group with Kuai and the like!
translations.idiomatic: As Han Xin left, he laughed and said, So this is what my life has come to: being counted with Fan Kuai.

sentences.zh: 上嘗從容與信言諸將能各有差。

translations.literal: The emperor once in relaxed manner discussed various generals with Xin, each having differences.
translations.idiomatic: In a relaxed conversation, the emperor once discussed with Han Xin the relative abilities of the generals.

sentences.zh: 上問曰：「如我，能將幾何？」

translations.literal: The emperor asked: As for me, how many troops can I command?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, As for me, how many troops can I command?

sentences.zh: 信曰：「陛下不過能將十萬。」

translations.literal: Xin said: Your Majesty can command no more than one hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, Your Majesty can command no more than a hundred thousand.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「如公何如？」

translations.literal: The emperor said: What about you, sir?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, And what about you?

translations.literal: He said:
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin replied:

sentences.zh: 如臣，多多益辦耳。」

translations.literal: As for this servant, the more the better handled.
translations.idiomatic: For me, the more the better.

sentences.zh: 上笑曰：「多多益辦，何為為我禽？」

translations.literal: The emperor laughed: If the more the better, why were you captured by me?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor laughed: If more is better for you, then why were you captured by me?

sentences.zh: 信曰：「陛下不能將兵，而善將將，此乃信之為陛下禽也。

translations.literal: Xin said: Your Majesty cannot command soldiers, but is good at commanding commanders. This is why Xin was captured by Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, Your Majesty does not excel at commanding common troops, but you excel at commanding commanders. That is why I was captured by you.

sentences.zh: 且陛下所謂天授，非人力也。」

translations.literal: Moreover, what Your Majesty has is what is called Heaven-bestowed, not human strength.
translations.idiomatic: What Your Majesty possesses is Heaven's gift, not merely human skill.

sentences.zh: 後陳豨為代相監邊，辭信，信挈其手，與步於庭數匝，仰天而嘆曰：「子可與言乎？

translations.literal: Later, when Chen Xi served as Chancellor of Dai supervising the border and took leave of Xin, Xin grasped his hand and walked with him in the courtyard several circuits, looked up to Heaven, and sighed: Can you and I speak?
translations.idiomatic: Later, when Chen Xi, serving in Dai, came to take leave, Han Xin took his hand and walked several turns in the courtyard, then looked up and sighed: Can we speak frankly?

sentences.zh: 吾欲與子有言。」

translations.literal: I wish to have words with you.
translations.idiomatic: I have something to tell you.

sentences.zh: 豨因曰：「唯將軍命。」

translations.literal: Chen Xi then said: I only await the general's command.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Xi said, I am ready to hear your instruction.

sentences.zh: 信曰：「公之所居，天下精兵處也，而公，陛下之信幸臣也。

translations.literal: Xin said: Where you are stationed is where the empire's elite troops are, and you are the emperor's trusted favored minister.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin said, Your post controls the empire's best troops, and you are one of the emperor's most trusted favorites.

sentences.zh: 人言公反，陛下必不信；

translations.literal: If one person says you rebel, the emperor surely will not believe.
translations.idiomatic: If one accusation says you rebel, the emperor will not believe it.

sentences.zh: 再至，陛下乃疑；

translations.literal: If it comes a second time, the emperor then will doubt.
translations.idiomatic: If a second accusation comes, he will begin to doubt.

sentences.zh: 三至，必怒而自將。

translations.literal: If it comes a third time, he will certainly be enraged and personally lead troops.
translations.idiomatic: If it comes a third time, he will be enraged and command the campaign himself.

sentences.zh: 吾為公從中起，天下可圖也。」

translations.literal: I will rise from within on your behalf, and the empire can be taken.
translations.idiomatic: I will rise from inside the capital in support of you, and then the empire can be seized.

sentences.zh: 陳豨素知其能，信之，曰：「謹奉教！」

translations.literal: Chen Xi had long known his abilities and trusted him, saying: I respectfully accept your teaching.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Xi had long respected Han Xin's ability and believed him. He said, I will respectfully follow your instruction.

sentences.zh: 漢十年，豨果反，高帝自將而往，信病不從。

translations.literal: In Han year ten, Xi indeed rebelled. Emperor Gao personally led troops and went. Xin was ill and did not follow.
translations.idiomatic: In the tenth year of Han, Chen Xi did rebel, and Emperor Gao marched personally. Han Xin claimed illness and did not accompany him.

sentences.zh: 陰使人之豨所，而與家臣謀，夜詐赦諸官徒奴，欲發兵襲呂后、太子。

translations.literal: He secretly sent someone to Xi's place, and with household retainers plotted: at night falsely proclaim amnesty for convicts and bond-servants of all offices, intending to raise troops and attack Empress Lu and the Crown Prince.
translations.idiomatic: Han Xin secretly sent someone to Chen Xi and plotted with his retainers to issue a fake nighttime amnesty for convicts and enslaved laborers, then raise troops to strike Empress Lu and the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 部署已定，待豨報。

translations.literal: Deployment was already fixed, awaiting Xi's report.
translations.idiomatic: The arrangements were in place, waiting only for word from Chen Xi.

sentences.zh: 其舍人得罪信，信囚，欲殺之。
